schools.summary: The chapter the great NT argument for the resurrection (Barnes), occasioned by some at Corinth denying it (Ellicott); Paul begins with the gospel they 'received and wherein ye stand' — firm ground for the case to come.
Chrysostom: he passes to the most necessary subject, the resurrection, in which they were greatly unsound.

By the gospel 'ye are saved' — its saving power conditioned on holding fast the message as preached; salvation begun and perfected in those who continue (Wesley); 'unless ye have believed in vain' — a faith that does not persevere, or rests on a false report, proves empty.

'First of all' (en protois, in importance not time); 'received' (parelabon) by direct revelation (cf 11:23) and faithfully delivered; 'Christ died for our sins' a vicarious, atoning death 'according to the scriptures' (Isa 53), the cross fulfilling the ancient word.

The burial proves 'the reality of His death' (no swoon); 'rose again' (egegertai, perfect — an abiding state, risen and remaining alive) 'according to the scriptures' (Ps 16:10) — the resurrection set within the gospel, foretold and fulfilled.
Cyril of Jerusalem: as the cross was verified, so verify the proofs of the Resurrection by the Scriptures.

'He appeared' (ophthe) — actual bodily appearance, not a vision; to Cephas first (Luke 24:34, perhaps a grace to the one who denied him), then 'the Twelve' (the standing title though the full number was not present); the resurrection established by eye-witnesses.

Over five hundred saw the risen Christ at once (likely the Galilean meeting, Matt 28:16); 'the greater part remain' invites the doubters to question the living witnesses — a multitude that could not be deceived together; 'fallen asleep' the Christian word for death, implying awakening.

'To James' (the Lord's brother, later the Jerusalem leader) — an appearance not in the Gospels but known early, perhaps explaining the brothers' presence in the upper room (Acts 1:14); 'to all the apostles' at the Ascension; the Lord appeared often in the forty days.

'Last of all' Christ appeared to Paul (Acts 9), the same risen Lord as the others, else his witness proved nothing; 'born out of due time' (ektroma, a miscarriage/untimely birth) — expressing the suddenness of his irregular call and, above all, his deep humility (Wesley: as an abortion unworthy the name of a man).

debates.question: What does 'born out of due time' (ektroma) signify?

sides.position: chiefly his humility — unworthy the name of apostle, as a miscarriage is unworthy the name of a man
the suddenness and violence of his untimely 'birth' into apostleship, called last and out of course

schools.summary: 'The least of the apostles,' unworthy the name — not from defect of commission but from his great sin in persecuting the church (cf Eph 3:8; 1 Tim 1:15); true humility, not false modesty; believers humbled all their lives even for pre-conversion sins (Wesley).

themes: not meet to be called an apostle

schools.summary: 'By the grace of God I am what I am' — all his attainments from grace, not merit; the grace 'not in vain' but fruitful, so he 'laboured more abundantly than they all' (sober fact), yet 'not I, but the grace of God' — grace neither idle nor self-produced.
Calvin: this overthrows free-will set against grace — Paul ascribes both the working and the willing to God.

themes: 'by the grace of God I am what I am'
'not I, but the grace of God'

schools.summary: 'Whether I or they, so we preach' — the united apostolic message; it matters not who preached, for all (greatest and least) proclaimed the same risen Christ with one mouth, and 'so ye believed' — the unity itself a proof, bringing the argument back to the main point.
Chrysostom: having abased then exalted himself to an equality with the apostles, he establishes his credit.

If Christ is preached as risen (and proved, vv1-11), it is illogical to deny 'resurrection of the dead'; the deniers (probably influenced by Greek philosophy) had not reckoned that denying resurrection in general denies Christ's — a reciprocal inference (Calvin), opening the chain of consequences.

The first inference, turned 'with tremendous force, but fair' — if no resurrection is possible at all, then Christ (truly dead and buried, sharing our flesh) is not risen either; the deniers cannot exempt Christ from their own rule, binding his rising to the doctrine they were abandoning.

If Christ is not risen, both preaching and faith are 'vain' (kenon, empty, void of content and power) — the resurrection the very substance of the message, not a detachable appendix; not a gospel with some error but 'an empty fallacy' (Calvin), nothing left to preach or believe.

If the dead rise not, the apostles are 'false witnesses of God' (pseudomartyres) — not honestly mistaken but deliberate deceivers (Ellicott), fabricating a lie and attributing it to God, who (on that view) did not raise Christ; no trivial absurdity (Calvin).

A deliberate, emphatic repetition of v13 — denying resurrection in general necessarily denies Christ's in particular; a great and momentous truth bearing restatement (Barnes), the hinge preparing the further, more personal consequences (vv17ff).

'Vain' here (mataia, false, a delusion — stronger than kenon of v14); 'ye are yet in your sins,' for the resurrection is God's seal on the atonement — a Christ still dead made no accepted expiation, the guilt unremoved; more than reformation was needed, an atonement attested by the rising (Wesley).

If Christ is not raised, those 'fallen asleep in Christ' (the Christian word for death, implying awakening) have 'perished' (apolonto, lost, their hope baseless) — even believers who died trusting, perhaps martyrs, gone to destruction; the denial robbing the dead of salvation.

'If in this life only we have hope... most pitiable' (eleeinoteroi) — Christians, having forgone the world's pleasures and borne persecution for a future that (on this view) does not exist, the saddest of all; better not to have believed (Calvin); the intolerable climax preparing 'but now.'

'But now' (nyni de) the logical triumph after the absurdities — Christ is risen indeed; 'firstfruits' (aparche, the sheaf pledging the harvest) — his rising the pledge, sample, and beginning of his people's resurrection, overturning every dark consequence.

The parallel of two representative men — death came 'by man' (Adam, whose sin brought mortality on all), resurrection 'by man' (Christ, the Second Adam, Rom 5:12); death not natural but from sin (Calvin); 'mortality by Adam, immortality by Christ' (Clarke), the remedy answering the ruin head for head.
The two solidarities — all 'in Adam' (the race) die, all 'in Christ' (united by faith) 'made alive' (zoopoiethesontai) by a power not their own; each head conveying his condition to his own.

debates.question: Does 'in Christ shall all be made alive' mean universal salvation?

sides.position: No — the 'all' in Christ are those united to him, the blessed resurrection of believers, matching the chapter's theme
Paul's thought on the second 'all' is not easy to press; the parallel is of heads, not a claim of universal salvation

schools.summary: 'In his own order' (tagma, a military rank/division) — Christ the firstfruits already risen, then 'they that are Christ's at his coming' (parousia, the King's royal advent); the interval not a denial but the orderly unfolding, checking impatience (Calvin).

themes: 'they that are Christ's at his coming'

schools.summary: 'Then cometh the end' (telos, the consummation) — Christ delivers up 'the kingdom' (the mediatorial kingdom, its purpose complete, not ceasing to reign as God) to the Father, having 'put down all rule and authority and power' (every intervening government made necessary by the fall), God's direct reign at last established.

'He must reign' — a moral necessity founded on Ps 110:1 ('till I make thine enemies thy footstool'); the reign continuing to its goal, the total subjugation of every foe; the conquest certain, the believer assured of final triumph.
Cyril of Jerusalem: 'till' does not mean Christ ceases to reign once his enemies are subdued — a perverse misreading; it marks the certainty of conquest, not the end of the kingship.

debates.question: Does 'till he hath put all enemies under his feet' mean Christ's reign ends?

sides.position: No — 'till' marks the certainty of the conquest, not the cessation of the kingship

schools.summary: Death the 'last enemy,' destroyed after Satan and sin, in the reverse order they prevailed (Wesley; Heb 2:14); abolished not by mere cessation but by the resurrection (Clarke) — so long as the dead lie in the grave, death reigns; the raising of the dead the crowning conquest.

God 'put all things under his feet' (Ps 8:6; Heb 2:8) — a comprehensive subjection of creation to Christ; but 'all things' self-evidently excepts the Father, who does the subjecting, guarding his supremacy in the economy of redemption even as the Son reigns over all.
Cyril of Jerusalem: the Father put all things in subjection to the Son, yet is himself excepted — the Son's lordship over all distinguished from the Father who grants it.

The Son's subjection not of his divine nature but the consummation of his mediatorial office — the incarnate Messiah presenting the completed kingdom to the Father; 'that God may be all in all,' the redeemed creation in perfect unmediated harmony with God, the final end of redemption.
Cyril of Jerusalem rebuts those who read the subjection as the Son 'absorbed into the Father' — an impious misreading; it concerns the economy, not the Son's eternal deity.

debates.question: How is the Son 'subject' to the Father (v28)?

sides.position: the consummation of his mediatorial office, not subordination of his deity nor absorption into the Father

themes: 'that God may be all in all'

schools.summary: Famously obscure ('the most difficult verse,' Clarke; 30+ interpretations, Robertson) — readings include baptism in prospect of death/martyrdom (Lightfoot), 'in the room of the dead' filling the slain's ranks (Wesley), or a lost Corinthian custom; however read, the practice presupposes the resurrection.
Chrysostom: Paul argues from the very things they practise, making the gainsayers witnesses by their own actions — the practice pointless if the dead rise not.

sides.position: baptism undergone in the prospect of death or martyrdom
baptized 'in the room of the dead,' converts filling the ranks of the fallen
a now-obscure Corinthian custom; the verse remains a puzzle with no certain reading

schools.summary: Paul presses his argument from the apostles' sufferings: why do 'we' (the apostles, the preachers) stand in jeopardy (danger, peril) every hour — constantly risking life — if there is no resurrection? Their willingness to face perpetual danger would be irrational folly unless there is a future life and reward; their conduct proves their conviction of the resurrection (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: it would be madness to expose oneself to continual death for the gospel if death ended all; the apostles' hazards testify to their hope beyond the grave.

themes: why stand we in jeopardy every hour
Paul's appeal to his constant peril
the apostles' perils evidence of the resurrection

schools.summary: Paul makes a solemn protestation (an asseveration, nearly an oath) by 'your rejoicing' — his glorying or boasting over them in Christ (his joy in them as his converts) — affirming the truth of what he says; and declares he 'dies daily,' daily exposed to death, living in constant danger and self-denial, dying to self and risking his life continually for the gospel (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: this daily dying confirms the previous argument — Paul's whole life of perpetual peril would be senseless without the hope of resurrection.

themes: I protest by your rejoicing which I have in Christ Jesus
Paul's solemn affirmation of daily dying

schools.summary: Lightfoot and Barnes lean to a literal sense — Paul was actually condemned to fight wild beasts in the arena at Ephesus, a real and deadly peril (though not recorded in Acts, the silence of the historian being no contradiction; cf. the unrecorded dangers of 2 Corinthians 11). The Epicurean maxim 'let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die' (Isaiah 22:13) is the only consistent course if the dead rise not — Paul citing not his own view but that of those who deny the resurrection.

Ellicott and Robertson (cautiously) take 'beasts' figuratively — fierce, savage men (the Ephesian opponents, the 'many adversaries' of 16:9); a Roman citizen would not lawfully be cast to beasts, and the event is absent from Acts and the peril-list of 2 Corinthians 11. Heraclitus called the Ephesians 'beasts'; the Psalms call opponents of God beasts (Psalm 22).

Clarke (with Wesley): figurative — Paul met brutish, savage men from whom he was in danger; he would have mentioned literal beasts in his catalogue of sufferings (2 Corinthians 11), and a Roman citizen was exempt from such a punishment; Tertullian and Chrysostom deny the literal sense.

debates.question: Did Paul literally fight wild beasts at Ephesus, or is 'beasts' figurative for savage men?

sides.position: Literal — Paul was actually condemned to fight wild beasts in the arena, a real peril unrecorded in Acts
Figurative — 'beasts' are fierce, savage men (the Ephesian opponents); a citizen would not be cast to beasts

themes: I have fought with beasts at Ephesus
let us eat and drink; for to morrow we die
Paul's peril and the folly of suffering without resurrection

schools.summary: Paul quotes a line from the Greek poet Menander (a current proverb): 'evil communications (bad company, corrupt associations) corrupt good manners (good morals, character)' — warning that fellowship with those who deny the resurrection will undermine sound faith and life; 'be not deceived' cautioning that their error is no harmless speculation but a corrupting influence (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: false doctrine, especially that which removes the hope of the resurrection and judgment, breeds loose living (as the Epicurean maxim shows); the company of such errorists is to be shunned.

themes: Be not deceived: evil communications corrupt good manners
the moral peril of false doctrine

schools.summary: Paul calls them to 'awake' (as from a drunken stupor or sleep) 'to righteousness' — to rouse themselves to sober, holy living, and to 'sin not,' abandoning the error and loose conduct; charging that 'some' among them are in deep ignorance of God, not truly knowing him whatever their pretensions, the denial of the resurrection betraying a fundamental ignorance of God's power and character (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: 'I speak this to your shame' is a sharp reproof — it is to their shame that such ignorance and error should exist among them, a church so endowed with gifts; Paul rebukes them to rouse them.

themes: Awake to righteousness, and sin not
some have not the knowledge of God: I speak this to your shame
the call to awake to righteousness

schools.summary: Paul anticipates the objector (perhaps a scoffer, or one perplexed): 'how' — by what means, what process — can the dead, long decayed, be raised at all? and 'with what body' — what kind of body will the risen have, since the old body has perished and dissolved? The objection presses both the possibility and the nature of the resurrection body (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Calvin): Paul will meet the objection not by philosophical proof but by analogies from nature (the seed) and by revelation, showing that God can and will give a fitting body.

themes: How are the dead raised up? and with what body do they come
the objection of how and with what body

schools.summary: Paul answers the objector sharply: 'thou fool' (senseless one) — the objection betrays folly, for the analogy of nature, daily before his eyes, answers it; the seed sown in the ground must first 'die' (decay, dissolve, lose its old form) before it is 'quickened' (made alive, springs up into new life); death is the very gateway to the new life, so it is no absurdity that the body must die to be raised (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: nature itself, in the dying and rising of the seed, refutes the objector; God's power to bring life out of death is daily displayed in the field.

themes: Thou fool, that which thou sowest is not quickened, except it die
the analogy of the seed that must die to live
death the gateway to new life

schools.summary: Paul develops the analogy: what is sown is not 'that body that shall be' (the full plant with stalk and ear) but 'bare grain' — a mere naked seed, of wheat or some other kind; the body that rises from it is far different from what was sown, so the risen body need not be identical in form or qualities to the body that was buried, as the plant differs gloriously from the bare seed (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the objector's difficulty (the dissolution of the body) is no obstacle, for God brings a glorious body from the buried seed, far surpassing what was sown.

themes: thou sowest not that body that shall be, but bare grain
the seed differs from the body that springs up
the risen body differs from the buried

schools.summary: Paul ascribes the whole to God: 'God giveth it a body as it hath pleased him' — the transformation of seed into plant is God's own work, according to his sovereign pleasure and wisdom; and 'to every seed his own body,' each kind of seed producing its own appropriate plant, there being order and fitness, each receiving the body suited to it (Barnes, Robertson).

Wesley: as God, by his power and wisdom, clothes each seed with its fitting body, so he can and will give the risen dead a fitting resurrection body — the difficulty of 'with what body?' answered by God's creative power.

themes: God giveth it a body as it hath pleased him
to every seed his own body
God gives each seed its fitting body
God's creative power the ground of the resurrection

schools.summary: Paul shows the variety in God's creation: 'all flesh is not the same' — there are different kinds of flesh, of men, of beasts, of fishes, of birds, each suited to its creature and element; since God has made so many kinds of flesh, each fitted to its purpose, it is no difficulty for him to make a new kind of body — a resurrection body — different from the present (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the manifold variety of bodies in creation proves God's power and freedom to give the risen a body of a different and higher kind.

themes: All flesh is not the same flesh
one kind of flesh of men, another of beasts, another of fishes, another of birds
the variety of flesh among creatures
God's freedom to make a new body

schools.summary: Paul extends the argument to 'celestial bodies' (the heavenly — sun, moon, stars) and 'bodies terrestrial' (the earthly — men, animals, plants); these are of different orders, with different kinds of glory, the heavenly having one kind of glory (splendour, brightness) and the earthly another; as God adorns each order with its own appropriate glory, so he can clothe the resurrection body with a new and higher glory (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: There are also celestial bodies, and bodies terrestrial
the glory of the celestial is one, and the glory of the terrestrial is another
the differing glory of heavenly and earthly bodies
a higher glory for the risen

schools.summary: Paul presses the variety further: even among the heavenly bodies there are degrees of glory — the sun has one glory, the moon another, the stars another, and 'one star differeth from another star in glory' (in brightness and splendour); so there may be a glory in the resurrection body far surpassing the present, and (some add) degrees of glory among the risen (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the manifold and graded glory of the heavens illustrates that God can bestow on the risen body a glory transcending all we now know.

themes: one glory of the sun, and another glory of the moon, and another glory of the stars
one star differeth from another star in glory
the differing glories of the heavenly lights
a surpassing glory for the risen

schools.summary: Paul now applies all the analogies (seed, flesh, glory) directly: 'so also' is the resurrection — the same principle of transformation and difference governs the rising of the dead; the body is 'sown' (buried) 'in corruption' (subject to decay, dissolution, putrefaction) and 'raised in incorruption' (imperishable, no longer liable to decay or death); the corruptible exchanged for the incorruptible (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: it is the same body that is sown and raised, but raised in a transformed, incorruptible state; not a different body but the same gloriously changed.

themes: So also is the resurrection of the dead
It is sown in corruption; it is raised in incorruption
the resurrection sown in corruption, raised in incorruption

schools.summary: The body is 'sown in dishonour' (the corpse, an object of corruption, laid away with grief) and 'raised in glory' (radiant, honoured, glorified); and 'sown in weakness' (helpless in death, powerless) and 'raised in power' (vigorous, strong, no longer subject to weakness); the resurrection body, far from the corruption, dishonour, and weakness of the buried corpse, will be glorious and powerful, fitted for the heavenly state (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the very body now laid in shame and weakness shall be raised in glory and power, transformed after the likeness of Christ's glorious body.

themes: It is sown in dishonour; it is raised in glory
it is sown in weakness; it is raised in power
the body raised in glory and power

schools.summary: The body is 'sown a natural (psychikon, animal) body' — endowed with animal life, sustained by breath, food, sleep, subject to the vital functions — and 'raised a spiritual (pneumatikon) body,' not a mere spirit but a body so governed by the Spirit as to be free of the animal functions, sustained as we conceive a spirit to be. Barnes denies that 'spiritual' means a refined or sublimated substance — it means a body without the vital functions; the identity of the body is preserved (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the SUBSTANCE of the body remains the same; only the QUALITY changes — there is no mention of substance here, and no change is made upon it; against those who, philosophizing, suppose the substance of the body will be spiritual. The present quality is called 'animal' (quickened by the soul), the future 'spiritual' (quickened by the Spirit), the energy of the Spirit being far more complete and exempt from the necessities of food and sleep.

debates.question: Does the 'spiritual body' mean a change in the body's substance or only in its quality?

sides.position: Only the quality changes; the substance of the body remains the same, its identity preserved
A body so spiritualized as to be like a spirit, without the vital functions (but not a change of substance into spirit)

themes: It is sown a natural body; it is raised a spiritual body
there is a natural body, and there is a spiritual body
the crux of substance and quality

schools.summary: Paul cites Genesis 2:7: the first Adam was made 'a living soul' (psychen zosan) — a being endowed with animal life, the head of the natural, earthly humanity, source of the natural body; while Christ, 'the last Adam,' was made 'a quickening (life-giving) spirit' — not merely possessing life, but the source of life to others, imparting spiritual and resurrection life, the head of the new, spiritual humanity (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: as the first Adam transmitted to his posterity the natural, animal body, so the last Adam (Christ) imparts to his the spiritual, life-giving body and life — the contrast grounding the two kinds of body.

themes: The first man Adam was made a living soul
the last Adam was made a quickening spirit
the contrast of the two Adams
Christ the source of spiritual life

schools.summary: Paul states the order of God's working: 'that was not first which is spiritual, but that which is natural' — the natural (animal) body and life came first (in Adam), and the spiritual afterward (in Christ and the resurrection); this is God's general order, the lower and natural preceding the higher and spiritual, first the earthly then the heavenly, first the body of humiliation then the body of glory (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: as in Adam we first bear the natural body, so only afterward, through Christ, do we attain the spiritual; the present natural state precedes the future glorious one.

themes: that was not first which is spiritual, but that which is natural
the order of natural then spiritual
God's pattern of lower before higher

schools.summary: The first man (Adam) was 'of the earth, earthy' — formed from the dust (Genesis 2:7), earthly in origin and nature, the head of the earthly humanity; the second man (Christ) is 'the Lord from heaven' — heavenly in origin and nature, the title marking his divine, pre-existent, heavenly origin in contrast to Adam's earthy one (Barnes, Robertson).

Chrysostom (with Calvin): as Adam was earthy and transmitted an earthy nature, so Christ is heavenly and imparts a heavenly nature; his being 'from heaven' testifies to his divine origin and his power to give the heavenly body.

themes: The first man is of the earth, earthy
the second man is the Lord from heaven

schools.summary: Paul draws the parallel: as is the earthy (Adam), so are all who are 'earthy' (his natural posterity) — sharing his earthly, mortal nature; and as is the heavenly (Christ), so are all who are 'heavenly' (his redeemed) — destined to share his heavenly nature and body; the two humanities, those in Adam bearing his earthly image, those in Christ his heavenly, each sharing the nature of his head (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley: as we now bear Adam's earthly, corruptible nature, so believers shall, in the resurrection, bear Christ's heavenly, glorious nature.

themes: As is the earthy, such are they also that are earthy
as is the heavenly, such are they also that are heavenly
men bearing the nature of their head

schools.summary: Believers have borne (and now bear) 'the image of the earthy' (Adam) — sharing his mortal, corruptible, natural body and likeness — and 'shall also bear the image of the heavenly' (Christ), in the resurrection conformed to his glorious body (Philippians 3:21), bearing his heavenly likeness; the certain hope of the redeemed (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Cyril): as surely as we now bear Adam's earthly image, so surely shall we bear Christ's heavenly image — the transformation grounded in our union with him.

themes: as we have borne the image of the earthy, we shall also bear the image of the heavenly
the promise of bearing Christ's image
the glorious destiny of the redeemed

schools.summary: 'Flesh and blood' is a Hebrew periphrasis for man in his present mortal, corruptible state — the animal body as now constituted, fragile, decaying, subject to death; such a body, unchanged, cannot enter the heavenly, incorruptible kingdom, and 'neither doth corruption inherit incorruption' restates that the corruptible cannot inherit the incorruptible state, a transformation being necessary (Barnes, Clarke, Robertson).

Calvin: our flesh will be a participant in the glory of God, but only as renewed and quickened by the Spirit of Christ; the corruptible cannot inherit God's incorruptible kingdom, so we must be renewed after Christ's image; Ellicott adds that the risen body is bloodless — Christ showed 'flesh and bones,' not 'flesh and blood,' blood being the type of the lower animal life.

themes: flesh and blood cannot inherit the kingdom of God

schools.summary: Paul announces a 'mystery' — a truth before hidden, now revealed: not all believers will 'sleep' (die) before Christ's coming (those alive at his return will not pass through death), but all (both the dead raised and the living) will 'be changed,' transformed into the incorruptible, spiritual body; the ancient manuscripts vary in the placement of the negative, but the sense Paul intends is that some will be alive and changed without dying (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry): the living at Christ's coming shall be instantly transformed, not needing to die first; all the redeemed, living and dead, shall alike be glorified.

themes: Behold, I shew you a mystery
We shall not all sleep, but we shall all be changed
the mystery of not all sleeping
the universal transformation of the redeemed

schools.summary: The transformation will be instantaneous — 'in a moment' (atomos, an indivisible instant) and 'the twinkling of an eye' — not a gradual process but a sudden, immediate change, announced by the 'last trumpet' (a figurative signal, as of an army or assembly, of the end and the resurrection); at its sound the dead are raised incorruptible (in the glorified body) and the living are changed (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the two effects occur together — the dead raised incorruptible and the living changed (transformed without dying), both simultaneously, at the trumpet's sound.

themes: In a moment, in the twinkling of an eye, at the last trump
the dead shall be raised incorruptible, and we shall be changed
the sudden change at the last trumpet

schools.summary: The 'must' (dei, necessity): 'this corruptible' (the present decaying body) 'must put on incorruption,' and 'this mortal' (the body subject to death) 'must put on immortality' — the transformation is indispensable for entering the heavenly state; the image of 'putting on' (as a garment) marking that the corruptible body is invested with new, imperishable qualities, not annihilated (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): it is 'this' same body — the very body now corruptible and mortal — that shall be clothed with incorruption and immortality; identity preserved through transformation.

themes: this corruptible must put on incorruption, and this mortal must put on immortality
the necessity of putting on incorruption
the body invested with imperishable life

schools.summary: Gill: at Christ's coming, both the living saints and the raised dead being clothed with incorruption and immortality, then shall the saying be fulfilled; Paul declaring that, when the corruptible has put on incorruption and the mortal immortality, then shall be fulfilled the saying 'Death is swallowed up in victory' — the triumph foretold (the transformation of the body at the resurrection accomplishing the prophet's word, Isaiah 25:8, death being utterly abolished, swallowed up and consumed in the victory of Christ over the grave, the victory Christ's, shared with his people, death defeated forever).

themes: this corruptible shall have put on incorruption
Death is swallowed up in victory

schools.summary: Gill: these words are taken from Hosea (13:14) and belong to the times of the Messiah, as the ancient Jews acknowledge (the Chaldee paraphrase interpreting of the Word of God); Barnes: this triumphant exclamation begins the fourth division of the chapter, the practical consequences of the doctrine; Paul breaking into triumphant song — 'O death, where is thy sting? O grave, where is thy victory?' — the believer's exultation over the conquered foes (death, once the king of terrors with its deadly sting, and the grave, once the victor over all flesh, now mocked as defeated, their sting drawn and their victory undone by the resurrection of Christ and his people).

themes: O death, where is thy sting?
O grave, where is thy victory?

schools.summary: Gill: death has a sting, and that is sin — sin is the cause of death (it entered the world by sin), gives it its resistless power (reaching all sorts of persons), and all its bitterness, agonies, and miseries; and the strength of sin is the law; Paul explaining that the sting of death is sin, and the strength of sin is the law — the diagnosis of death's power (what makes death deadly, its sting, is sin, for death is the wages of sin, Romans 6:23, and without sin death would have no terror; and what gives sin its force and condemning power, its strength, is the law, which sin violates and which pronounces the curse), so that Christ's victory over sin, by his atonement, and the law, by fulfilling and bearing its curse, is the root of the victory over death.

debates.question: How is 'the strength of sin the law'?

sides.position: The law gives sin its force and condemning power — sin violates the law, and the law pronounces the curse/condemnation, so without the law sin would not condemn; Christ's bearing the law's curse breaks sin's strength

themes: the sting of death is sin
the strength of sin is the law
Christ's victory over sin and the law

schools.summary: Gill: the victory is over sin (the sting of death), the law (the strength of sin), and death and the grave — the ground of the triumphant song, given through our Lord Jesus Christ; Paul giving thanks to God, who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ — the ascription of the triumph to God's grace in Christ (the victory over sin, the law, death, and the grave is not won by ourselves but given to us freely by God, through the person and work of Christ, his atonement and resurrection), so that all the glory of the conquest belongs to God, the thanksgiving the ground of the triumphant song (v.55).

themes: the triumph ascribed to God's grace

schools.summary: Matthew Henry draws the practical use of the doctrine; Gill: Paul concludes the whole, addressing the saints at Corinth tenderly (owning their spiritual relation and his love), that they might be sound in principle and right in practice and continue so; Paul concluding with the exhortation — be steadfast, unmovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing their labour is not in vain in the Lord — the practical fruit of the resurrection hope (because the dead rise and the victory is sure, the believers should be firmly grounded in the faith, steadfast and unmovable against error, diligent and overflowing in the Lord's service, assured that no labour for Christ is wasted but shall be richly rewarded in the resurrection).

themes: always abounding in the work of the Lord
your labour is not in vain in the Lord
the practical fruit of the resurrection hope
